Questions.Text: A company is switching from a predictive to an agile project management approach. The team is challenged with changing customer requirements. What is the best corrective action for this issue?
Options: Use the project retrospectives
Provide the product owner with training and acceptance training
Develop guidelines for training and mentoring
Breakdown complex story points
Questions.Explanation: Break down complex story points Staffing challenges - Develop guidelines for training and mentoring. Stakeholder engagement - Provide the product owner with training and acceptance training. Changing customer requirements - Breakdown complex story points Facilitate organizational learning - Use the project retrospectives.

Questions.Text: An experienced project manager joins an ongoing project's team and wants to estimate the amount of time required to tackle a number of complex tasks. The team uses a Kanban board but is open to new ideas. The project manager suggests using story points. What is the first thing the team should do before adopting this new approach?
Options: Calculate the story points only for new backlog items.
Calculate the story points required for everything on the task board.
Convert all work in progress limits to story points.
Measure story points over several sprints.
Questions.Explanation: Convert all work in progress limits to story points In switching from a Kanban board to story points in an ongoing project, the team should first convert work in progress to story points. The other answer choices are incorrect. Backlog items and other task board items can be converted afterwards, after the team understands how Kanban tasks translate to story points; measuring story points over sprints can occur only after the team starts using sprints.

Questions.Text: A new project has been assigned to an existing agile team. The previous project the team worked on was so successful that a senior stakeholder is requesting that the team use the same governance used in the previous project. What should the project manager do next?
Options: Confer with the project management office (PMO) to gain their insight on governance for the project.
Work with senior managers to adapt existing governance.
Inform senior management that the same project governance can be used on the new effort.
Meet with the product owner to determine what project governance to use.
Questions.Explanation: Work with senior managers to adapt existing governance Regardless of Agile or even waterfall, the governance for an effort needs to be tailored to the specific needs of the project, stakeholders, customers etc.

Questions.Text: For an agile project, the project manager does not have a 100% dedicated team. How should the project manager ensure good velocity, cycle duration, and on-time iteration completion? (Choose two)
Options: Request that members log their time to guarantee the percentage of time working on assignments.
Request the product owner and stakeholders to provide cross-functional team members.
Get buy-in from the product owner and stakeholders for obtaining full-time team members.
Revisit the resource log to ensure the most skilled resources are on the project.
Deploy additional team members, irrespective of the story points or iterations.
Questions.Explanation: Request the product owner and stakeholders to provide cross-functional team members and Get buy-in from the product owner and stakeholders for obtaining full-time team members. Dedicated team members are highly recommended in agile project development. The project manager should collaborate with functional managers to release their employees to contribute effectively and gain buy-in from team members that are cross-functional and available. The other answer choices are incorrect. Requesting that members log their time to guarantee the percentage of time working on assignments does not guarantee that the team member will prioritize their time correctly and will take time to switch between assignments delaying the delivery. Revisiting the resource log to look for more resources does not help in this case. Deploying additional team members will be counterproductive and may increase cycle durations and reduce velocity.

Questions.Text: The government of a country intends to construct a multibillion-dollar railway across three major cities. The designs for the project will be completed within a few months after signing the contract. However, the government has an urgent need and expects the project deliverables to be achieved within 24 months. Which two techniques should the project manager consider when developing the project management plan in order to evenly share the risk to both parties? (Choose two)
Options: Fixed-price increments
Not-to-exceed time
Early cancellation option
Dynamic scope option
Graduated time and materials
Questions.Explanation: Fixed-price increments and Graduated time and materials Fixed-price increment allows the project to be divided in phases (cities) in order to ensure control of the project. Graduated time and material shares the risk between both parties to the contract. Incentives are rewarded to the contractors for meeting early deadline and damages arising as a result of delay. The other answer choices are incorrect. Not-to-exceed time, early cancellation option, and dynamic scope option do not share the risk with the two parties.

Questions.Text: A project manager has just taken over an ongoing project from another project manager. During a review of the project management plan, the project manager realizes that the cost variance and schedule variance are largely negative. Several issues reported on the project have not been resolved, vendors have not been paid for work done, and mitigation of secondary risks is quickly depleting the budget. Which two actions should have been done to avoid this situation? (Choose two)
Options: Implement clear assumptions in the project charter
Add a contingency budget to the project charter
Identify high-level risks and responses in the project planning phase
Identify project handoff procedures
Ensure that the project governance controls were approved and implemented
Questions.Explanation: Implement clear assumptions in the project charter and Identify high-level risks and responses in the project planning phase In order to avoid this situation, all assumptions and risks should be documented. The other answer choices are incorrect. Handoff procedures and updating project controls would not have solved any of the project issues.

Questions.Text: During the project closing, the project sponsor informs the project manager that one of the deliverables, developed by a contractor, must be transferred to a nonprofit organization at no cost. The procurement documentation states that the property of this deliverable reverts to the contractor once the procurement contract is finished. What should the project manager do?
Options: Add "avoid out of scope agreements during the closure process" to the lessons learned register.
Initiate a change request and execute the integrated change control process.
Consider this transfer as an open issue for the contract department and continue closing the project.
Review the contract agreement with the contractor in order to modify the property of the deliverable.
Questions.Explanation: Consider this transfer as an open issue for the contract department and continue closing the project. Outside contracts are not in scope for this project. The project manager should continue with project closure activities and the transfer of the contract should be taken up by the contracts department.

Questions.Text: A project manager is appointed to design and develop a strategy for a government organization. The project manager recognizes that the project has a limited amount of detailed information to prepare project estimates. However, several other government organizations have already successfully adopted the same strategy. In this scenario, which type of estimating method should the project manager select?
Options: Parametric modelling
Analogous estimation
Three-point estimating
Expert judgment
Questions.Explanation: Expert judgment Expert judgment is the best option because it is using the project manager's direct experience. It tends to be quick and easy and can be used for creating top-down or bottom-up estimates. The information needed for the other types of estimating methods may not be available for this type of contract. Expert Judgment is a technique in which judgment is provided based upon a specific set of criteria and/or expertise that has been acquired in a specific knowledge area, application area, or product area, a particular discipline, an industry, etc. Such expertise may be provided by any group or person with specialized education, knowledge, skill, experience, or training.[1]. This knowledge base can be provided by a member of the project team, or multiple members of the project team, or by a team leader or team leaders. However, typically expert judgment requires an expertise that is not present within the project team and, as such, it is common for an external group or person with a specific relevant skill set or knowledge base to be brought in for a consultation, ]

Questions.Text: An urban hotel design project has an international, multidisciplinary, and cross-cultural team. The project manager is in the process of developing the first version of the project management plan. What should the project manager do?
Options: Allow sufficient time for the project team to prepare the project management plan before the project execution stage starts.
Keep the project management plan summarized and at a high level to facilitate communication among the diverse team members.
Separate the risk register from the project management plan, due to the project's complexity, making it a standalone document.
Include the project budget and project cash flow scenarios as part of the project management plan.
Questions.Explanation: Include the project budget and project cash flow scenarios as part of the project management plan. The project manager should ensure that the first version of the project management plan includes high level scope, timelines, and project budget. The other answer choices are incorrect. The project planning is an iterative process and is developed and incremented in stages. Providing sufficient time for completion of the project management plan will only delay the remaining phases of the project. Keeping the project management plan summarized and at a high level is a good option for project communication only if the project constraints (scope, cost and time) are well defined. Hence this option is not the most appropriate answer. The risk management plan will always be part of a project management plan and cannot be separated. Hence this option is not the correct answer.

Questions.Text: A project manager in a quickly growing hybrid organization is becoming increasingly frustrated at the rapid amount of change in daily operations. Several newer team members have already left the team or have been promoted to other roles in the organization. What should the project manager do to ensure the project governance remains stable?
Options: Work with senior leadership to update the organizational process assets (OPAs).
Develop a new social contract to empower the team.
Update the team charter with new team members.
Update the project management plan on a regular basis.
Questions.Explanation: Develop a new social contract to empower the team. The project manager should develop a social contract and empower the team to decide how they will work together. The other answer choices are incorrect. Working with senior management to update OPAs will not help project governance to be stable. Simply updating the team charter or project management plan will also not help to ensure project governance remains stable.

Questions.Text: The project sponsor for a major initiative recently sent a memo demanding that a milestone for a key deliverable be met much earlier than anticipated. Although this can be achieved, the original training schedule for the small project team will be dramatically impacted. The trainer will be available when needed. What should the project manager do to meet the updated timeline milestone?
Options: Organize one-on-one workshops with the trainer and each member of the project team.
Organize a workshop with the trainer and project team and then conduct several internal team review sessions.
Organize a series of workshops with the trainer and project team over the shorter timeframe.
Organize a dedicated, longer workshop in the schedule with the trainer and project team well in advance of the new deadline.
Questions.Explanation: Organize a workshop with the trainer and project team and then conduct several internal team review sessions. In order to meet the updated timeline milestone, the project manager should organize a workshop with the trainer and project team and then conduct several internal team review sessions. This will empower the team to problem solve together, rather than focusing on the capabilities of one individual. The other answer choices are incorrect. Organizing one-on-one workshops is not the most cost-effective approach and does not empower the team; organizing a dedicated, longer workshop does not empower the team and adds to cost.

Questions.Text: Company B just launched a new product across international regions and now poses a business threat to company A. Company A must launch a product to respond to this threat. As part of an agile approach, company A's team chooses region X as the baseline to test the first iteration. While the product meets standards and customer requirements in region X, it does not comply with two of the other regions' standards. What should the project manager from company A do?
Options: Ensure correctness for the applicable region's product and plan future iterations outside of this project.
Document this as a noncompliance in the quality management plan and correct it in the next iteration.
Re-plan all of the iterations to meet all three regions' standards within the scope of the work.
Connect with customers from each region to reach an agreement on the existing design.
Questions.Explanation: Ensure correctness for the applicable region's product and plan future iterations outside of this project. An agile approach refines work items and delivers them frequently. It also assures controlling quality to comply with the requirements throughout the project. In the case of noncompliance, the project manager should redefine the scope of the current project, followed by launching in other regions, complying with standards, in future projects.

Questions.Text: A functional manager involved in a project complains about the latest quality metrics reports associated with all previous iterations. The team does not think this is a valid complaint. What should the project manager do first?
Options: Fix the reports to accommodate the functional manager's complaints.
Speak to the functional manager's superior to check if this is a common complaint.
Review the RACI matrix to identify who is responsible for quality metrics.
Perform a quality audit to validate the applied quality metrics.
Questions.Explanation: Review the RACI matrix to identify who is responsible for quality metrics. The project manager should first consult the RACI matrix to identify who is responsible for quality metrics. Responsibilities, including quality management activities, are identified within the RACI matrix. The other answer choices are incorrect because they deflect responsibility or are not the first best thing the project manager should do to find out the root cause of the complaints.

Questions.Text: A project manager has been asked to manage an existing complex project. Some goals have already been delivered by the previous project manager. While reviewing the project management plan, the project manager realizes that one of the key deliverables is missing from the project goal. What should the project manager do next to ensure the expected project value is delivered?
Options: Escalate the issue about the deliverable to the project sponsor.
Confirm the expected deliverables and rebaseline the project.
Track the deliverable by reviewing the requirements traceability matrix.
Use the contingency fund for more resources to complete the missing deliverable.
Questions.Explanation: Track the deliverable by reviewing the requirements traceability matrix. The project manager should use the requirements traceability matrix to ensure that the expected project value is delivered. A requirement is a condition or capability that is necessary to be present in a product, service, or result to satisfy a business need. A requirements traceability matrix links product requirement from their origin to the deliverables that satisfy them. Requirements can evolve over time, so to ensure the expected project value is delivered, the project manager should track the requirement by reviewing the requirements traceability matrix.

Questions.Text: A lengthy upgrade project is being delivered in a series of phased outputs. The end users will face some level of disruption during each phase prior to the outputs being achieved. What should the project manager do to ensure that the intended value is being delivered at each phase?
Options: Gather feedback using surveys of the end user experience and analyze the results.
Review the actual costs incurred to ensure that the project remains on budget.
Validate the scope to ensure that the required quality of outputs is being met.
Track progress toward each benefit, based on the benefits management plan.
Questions.Explanation: Track progress toward each benefit, based on the benefits management plan. In order to ensure that the intended value is being delivered or on track to be delivered, the project manager should track progress toward each benefit, based on the benefits management plan. The other answer choices are incorrect because they do not mention the benefits management plan. The benefits must be identified, and measurements put in place to track progress towards the benefits.

Questions.Text: A project team is completing backlog items within a four-week sprint. One of the key team members will be away for one week and will be unable to complete the tasks in progress. What should the project manager do?
Options: Support a knowledge transfer between the team member who will be away and the other project team members.
Add a temporary resource to the team to help increase velocity and complete the items for this sprint.
Remove a few items from the current sprint with agreement from the product owner.
Increase the sprint duration so the team can complete all the planned items and present them to the product owner.
Questions.Explanation: Remove a few items from the current sprint with agreement from the product owner. The project manager does not determine the length of the sprint; this is determined by the product owner. Product owner concurrence is required to modify sprint duration; thus the project manager should seek approval from the product owner to remove a few items from the current sprint. The other answer choices are incorrect. Adding a team member may slow down velocity; increasing the sprint duration may not be in the best interest of the product owner; and there is no indication that knowledge transfer is not already occurring.

Questions.Text: A customer is upset because a critical issue for a project deliverable has not been resolved by the agile team, even though troubleshooting has been running for several weeks. During the daily meetings, the team complains about the technical challenge and their lack of knowledge to resolve the issue. Which two actions should the project manager take to resolve this situation? (Choose two)
Options: Escalate the case to a functional manager to bring a technical expert to the team.
Explain the situation to the customer and include an action plan.
Keep the team working on the sprint backlog in order to accomplish the next release milestone.
Update the issue and risk logs, including the action plan to resolve the issue.
Set up a new release date for this deliverable with the product owner.
Questions.Explanation: Escalate the case to a functional manager to bring a technical expert to the team and explain the situation to the customer and include an action plan. Considering that the customer is already upset and that the team has not been able to solve the situation, the way to solve this will be to get help from outside the team and explaining the plan to the customer. The other answer choices are incorrect. Continuing to work without a solution is not the best approach as the issue is still looming; updating the issue and risk logs would have already happened since the beginning of the issue a few weeks ago; unless they know how to solve the issue, they will be unable to forecast an accurate release date.

Questions.Text: A project is challenged by a multitude of problems resulting in defects and stakeholder complaints. The project manager has been contemplating a quick and effective approach to address the problems. Which two actions should the project manager take? (Choose two)
Options: Gather the project team members and have a brainstorming session.
Refer to the organizational process assets (OPAs) to assess lessons learned.
Use a trend analysis to engage some of the project team members.
Use an Ishikawa diagram to help identify the problems.
Use a Pareto chart to identify how the challenges of the project should be addressed.
Questions.Explanation: Use an Ishikawa diagram to help identify the problems and Use a Pareto chart to identify how the challenges of the project should be addressed. The Ishikawa/fish-bone diagram can be used in a systematic identification of problems as well as their origins. The output of this exercise could be used to develop the Pareto chart to indicate the 20% of the cause problems which when addressed would eliminate 80% of current challenges.

Questions.Text: A new project manager has taken over an ongoing project. Progress reports show that the project is within the allocated budget and schedule. However, a key supplier has indicated that they will not be able to supply critical materials as scheduled. What should the project manager do first?
Options: Submit a change request to change control board (CCB).
Review the performance against the project baseline.
Take legal action against the defaulting supplier.
Document and update the risk register.
Questions.Explanation: Review the performance against the project baseline. The project manager should first investigate the performance against the project baseline to investigate and determine if the delay of the critical materials will impact the project schedule. The other answer choices are incorrect. There is no change requests raised by the key supplier or project, so there's no need to submit the change request to the CCB. The type of contact is not provided in the question, so there's no sufficient information that legal action is required to be taken to the supplier for the late delivery of the critical materials. The project manager should understand the issue and impact on the actual situation before updating the risk register.

Questions.Text: In the middle of an iteration, a team member suggests a new method of doing things. The method provides no value to the end customer; however it may help the team to work more efficiently. The change may require several days of work to implement. How should the project manager respond to the suggestion?
Options: The change should be considered because it will help the team work more efficiently.
The change should not be considered because it adds no value to the end customer.
The change should be considered and presented to the project sponsor for approval.
The change should be considered and implemented in the next sprint.
Questions.Explanation: The change should be considered and implemented in the next sprint. All changes are welcome and will be evaluated by the team. Changes will be considered immediately for further action, but will be implemented in the next sprint.

Questions.Text: A new product was accepted by the customer, but after two weeks the project manager receives a complaint from the customer saying that the product's functionality is incomplete. What should the project manager do first?
Options: Request an inspection of the product.
Validate the scope with the customer.
Escalate the issue to the project sponsor.
Review the Validate Scope process.
Questions.Explanation: Validate the scope with the customer. It is necessary to validate the scope with the customer to clarify that the objectives were delivered. The other answer choices are incorrect. The inspection of the production should only be done after the validation of the scope with the customer is completed. The escalation of the issue to the project sponsor should only be done after the initial investigation and inspection is completed. The review of the Validate Scope process is not the first thing that the project manager should do. This should only be done after understanding the situation of the complaint.

Questions.Text: A serious quality issue has been identified during the implementation of a major public infrastructure project. Fixing the issue will cause a substantial delay to the committed project completion date. What should the project manager do?
Options: Seek a schedule extension from the project sponsor.
Ask the engineering team to find a solution to fix the issue.
Update the issue log and continue monitoring the risk.
Engage all stakeholders and update lessons learned.
Questions.Explanation: Engage all stakeholders and update lessons learned. The communication plan should be constantly updated to include all interested parties. As it is a major public project the general public should be informed if there is any serious issue. Seeking the schedule extension from the project sponsor and asking the engineering team to find a solution to fix the issue are something the project manager can do after engaging all stakeholders first on the issue. As the quality issue is serious, just updating the issue log and monitoring the risk is not sufficient.

Questions.Text: During the standup, a team member reported the completion of a story which is a dependency for other stories. When the team starts working on the related stories, they cannot continue with development because the initial story is not finished. What should the project manager do to correct the situation?
Options: Review the sprint planning with the whole team to understand story interdependencies.
Review the acceptance criteria with the team every time a story is pulled into the sprint.
Ask the product owner to review the acceptance criteria before pushing it into the sprint backlog.
Ask the team to explain their interpretation of definition of done (DoD) at the end of the sprint.
Questions.Explanation: Review the acceptance criteria with the team every time a story is pulled into the sprint. Identify the root cause analysis as a Scrum master to avoid misunderstanding within the team moving forward not tactic. Acceptance criteria is the root of the problem, it wasn't clear but it wasn't discussed. Definition of Done (DoD) is a list of requirements that a user story must adhere to for the team to call it complete (macro). While the Acceptance Criteria of a User Story consist of set of Test Scenarios that are to be met to confirm that the software is working as expected (micro). The difference between these two is that the DoD is common for all the User Stories whereas the Acceptance Criteria is applicable to specific User Story. Acceptance Criteria of each User Story will be different based on the requirements of that User Story. In other words, Both DoD and Acceptance Criteria must be met in order to complete the User Story.  The Product Increment is not considered to be complete, unless both these two lists are done.

Questions.Text: In a review meeting, the team detects a misunderstanding about one critical deliverable. What should the project manager do to resolve this misunderstanding?
Options: Ask the team to discuss the issue and reach a consensus.
Ask the customer to clarify the deliverable.
Ask the product owner to clarify the deliverable.
Ask the sponsor to clarify the deliverable.
Questions.Explanation: Ask the team to discuss the issue and reach a consensus. Team consensus on the misunderstanding of the deliverable is needed to proceed to the appropriate solution. PMBoK Chapter 5 and 8 state: "Project life cycles can range along a continuum from predictive approaches at one end to adaptive or agile approaches at the other. In a predictive life cycle, the project deliverables are defined at the beginning of the project and any changes to the scope are progressively managed. In an adaptive or agile life cycle, the deliverables are developed over multiple iterations where a detailed scope is defined and approved for each iteration when it begins. 5.5 VALIDATE SCOPE Validate Scope is the process of formalizing acceptance of the completed project deliverables. The key benefit of this process is that it brings objectivity to the acceptance process and increases the probability of final product, service, or result acceptance by validating each deliverable. This process is performed periodically throughout the project as needed. 5.5.1.3 VERIFIED DELIVERABLES Verified deliverables are project deliverables that are completed and checked for correctness through the Control Quality process. 5.7 CONTROL QUALITY Control Quality is the process of monitoring and recording results of executing the quality management activities to assess performance and ensure the project outputs are complete, correct, and meet customer expectations. The key benefit of this process is verifying that project deliverables and work meet the requirements specified by key stakeholders for final acceptance. This process is performed throughout the project 8.3 CONTROL QUALITY Control Quality is the process of monitoring and recording results of executing the quality management activities in order to assess performance and ensure the project outputs are complete, correct, and meet customer expectations. The key benefit of this process is verifying that project deliverables and work meet the requirements specified by key stakeholders for final acceptance. The Control Quality process determines if the project outputs do what they were intended to do. Those outputs need to comply with all applicable standards, requirements, regulations, and specifications. This process is performed throughout the project. CONSIDERATIONS FOR AGILE/ADAPTIVE ENVIRONMENTS In order to navigate changes, agile methods call for frequent quality and review steps built in throughout the project rather than toward the end of the project. Recurring retrospectives regularly check on the effectiveness of the quality processes. They look for the root cause of issues then suggest trials of new approaches to improve quality. Subsequent retrospectives evaluate any trial processes to determine if they are working and should be continued or new adjusting or should be dropped from use. In order to facilitate frequent, incremental delivery, agile methods focus on small batches of work, incorporating as many elements of project deliverables as possible. Small batch systems aim to uncover inconsistencies and quality issues earlier in the project life cycle when the overall costs of change are lower." This explanation justifies that the correct answer to this question is: "The team to discuss and reach a consensus." The Distractors are concise but are well worded and accepted.

Questions.Text: A project manager has been assigned to a new project with multiple stakeholders. Each stakeholder has provided their expectations for the project, and the project manager realizes some of the expectations conflict. What should the project manager do?
Options: Ensure the goals and expectations of the key stakeholders are met.
Review the conflict management plan for the stakeholders.
Update the stakeholder register with the stakeholders' expectations.
Create a project hierarchy to manage the stakeholders' expectations.
Questions.Explanation: Ensure the goals and expectations of the key stakeholders are met. Properly identifying and prioritizing project stakeholders will help the project manager manage expectations and objectives of the project. PMBoK Sixth Edition states: "The project management team seeks input and encourages involvement from relevant stakeholders while planning the project and developing the project management plan and project documents. 3.24 PLAN STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT Plan Stakeholder Engagement is the process of developing approaches to involve project stakeholders based on their needs, expectations, interests, and potential impact on the project. The key benefit is that it provides an actionable plan to interact with stakeholders effectively. This process is performed periodically throughout the project as needed. 4.1.2.3 INTERPERSONAL AND TEAM SKILLS Interpersonal and team skills that can be used for this process include but are not limited to: Conflict management. Conflict management can be used to help bring stakeholders into alignment on the objectives, success criteria, high-level requirements, project description, summary milestones, and other elements of the charter"

Questions.Text: A supplier and their client agree on 80% of the scope for a project contract they are working on for the delivery of marketing content. The client has a fixed budget and target completion date and wants to get the most business value from the project by increasing the scope based on a best-effort delivery close to the end of the project. What should the project manager do?
Options: Create the contract based on the agreed scope and plan an addendum for the scope changes.
Ensure that a change control process is documented in the contract.
Propose a dynamic scope contract option and add a fixed number of iterations.
Increase the number of resources in the contract to deliver the requirements.
Questions.Explanation: Propose a dynamic scope contract option and add a fixed number of iterations. Best-effort changes at the end of a project can be delivered at a fixed cost by specifying a fixed number of increments where additional scope can be prioritized. Strict change control or addendums would risk missing the completion date. Adding resources is not necessary if the scope is on best-effort basis.

Questions.Text: During an iteration, the project manager realizes that one of the product features will be impacted by the resignation of a key project team member. What should the project manager do to address this situation?
Options: Submit a change request to modify the delivery date based on this unexpected situation.
Send a proposal to human resources (HR) asking to them to retain the key member until the end of the project.
Accelerate knowledge sharing with the cross-functional team to ensure that this situation is understood.
Discuss the situation with the product owner and determine if resources should be added to the team.
Questions.Explanation: Discuss the situation with the product owner and determine if resources should be added to the team. Project managers must discuss issues as they arise with product owners and the larger team to secure buy-in for issue resolution.

Questions.Text: A highly skilled subject matter expert (SME) is working on a government project. About 30% of the deliverables will be executed based on an agile approach, and the SME is asked to join the team. However, the SME does not want to work on the agile iterations because working on a team demotivates and slows the SME down. The SME mainly wants to achieve the highest-quality output possible. Which approach should the project manager take to reconcile the SME's skills and motivation as well as the agile team structure and project goals?
Options: Recommend that the SME develop more emotional intelligence by integrating with the team.
Propose that the SME combine the product owner and team facilitator roles.
Request that the SME conduct the retrospective meetings as an objective observer.
Require the SME to receive mentoring to improve their attitude.
Questions.Explanation: Propose that the SME combine the product owner and team facilitator roles. Given the SME's level of expertise and commitment to quality, that person would be an ideal product owner and would empower the team as facilitator. This empowerment is aligned with Agile principles. The SME would also provide better continuity between the predictive and incremental project phases.

Questions.Text: During a standup meeting, some of the team members strongly disagree about how tasks have been distributed. They feel tired and frustrated because task distribution has been a frequent complaint throughout the project. What should the project manager do?
Options: Review the project management plan to determine how to manage this specific situation.
Discuss with other project managers if they had to address similar complaints in their projects.
Negotiate with the team to find a new way to distribute the work of each iteration scope.
Interview these members to identify, assess, and manage their feelings about the situation.
Questions.Explanation: Interview these members to identify, assess, and manage their feelings about the situation. Every project manager needs to have the ability to identify, assess, and manage the personal emotions of oneself and other people, as well as the collective emotions of groups of people.

Questions.Text: A company is working on a hybrid project in which the deliverables have quality issues at the end of one of the sprints. Which action should the project manager take?
Options: Review the definition of done (DoD).
Look for a clear set of working agreements.
Apply value stream mapping.
Use a plan-driven approach.
Questions.Explanation: Look for a clear set of working agreements. Cross-functional teams consist of team members with all the skills necessary to produce a working product. In software development, cross-functional teams are typically comprised of designers, developers, testers, and any other required roles. The cross-functional development teams consist of professionals who deliver potentially releasable product on a regular cadence. Cross-functional teams are critical because they can deliver finished work in the shortest possible time, with higher quality, without external dependencies.

Questions.Text: A new agile team is forming and the project manager notices that the team is struggling in many areas. The team is experiencing both the inability to meet iteration commitments and team member conflicts. What should the project manager do next?
Options: Involve management to help resolve the conflicts.
Inform management that commitments will not be met.
Raise the issues in the retrospective and propose solutions.
Coach the team both as a whole and individually.
Questions.Explanation: Coach the team both as a whole and individually The project manager should coach the team both as a whole and individually because coaching helps the team’s performance get better, in a series of steps. The other answer choices are incorrect. Involving management is not going to help the team to meet the iteration commitment not resolve the conflicts. Informing management that commitments will not be met is not the best choice because as a project manager it is required to inform the management about missing the commitment, but in agile teams, coaching is the best thing to do instead of directly informing to the management. Raising the issues is not the best choice as waiting until the retrospective is too late.

Questions.Text: A team member with sufficient experience and knowledge informs the project manager about a conflict with another team member and asks for advice and help. What should the project manager do?
Options: The project manager discusses the issue with the team members, and they decide on the solution together.
The project manager and team members discuss the challenge, and the project manager chooses the solution.
The project manager and team members discuss the situation, and the project manager directs the steps to be taken.
The project manager and the team members discuss the situation, and the project manager leaves the decision to the team members.
Questions.Explanation: The project manager discusses the issue with the team members, and they decide on the solution together Collaborative planning and decision making leads to team member ownership and buy-in, which results in higher motivation, which in turn will more than compensate for the time and effort required to arrive at agreement. The project manager should discuss the issue with the team members, and they decide on the solution together: Leadership with Buy-in

Questions.Text: A new project manager joins an organization that has never had formal project management processes. Some team members refuse to participate in basic tasks such as taking ownership of action items at meetings. What should the project manager do?
Options: Set up a private meeting with the specific team members to discuss the benefits of project management.
Create an internal document outlining the benefits of project management for all team members.
Explore the benefits of project management with executives of the organization.
Host a meeting with the team focused on collaborative problem solving to develop an approach to accomplishing project tasks.
Questions.Explanation: Host a meeting with the team focused on collaborative problem solving to develop an approach to accomplishing project tasks The project manager should use collaborative problem solving to allow for input from all team members without involving executive mandates, which allows for more buy-in from the team to accomplish the tasks. The other answer choices are incorrect. Setting up a meeting with individual project team members does not provide the opportunity for team buy-in; creating an internal document does not ensure buy-in from team members or create a team culture that the team is part of the problem solving process; exploring the benefits of project management process with executives is a top down approach and is not the first approach a project manager would take to achieve buy-in.

Questions.Text: A project team realizes that the product's architecture will not support the growing number of users. What should the project manager do?
Options: Assign a team member to execute performance testing, making sure that the product's performance is acceptable.
Hire external experts to assess the product architecture and come up with a resolution plan.
Use the next retrospective session to evaluate the problem with the team and prioritize the solution on the product backlog.
Negotiate with the stakeholders to slow down product development until a viable solution is available.
Questions.Explanation: Use the next retrospective session to evaluate the problem with the team and prioritize the solution on the product backlog The project team is usually best equipped to solve the issues and the retrospective is an appropriate time to discuss issues and develop solutions. Without the teams input on the issue/solution it is premature to hire experts, perform additional testing, and/or negotiate with stakeholders

Questions.Text: During initial project planning, a project manager was informed that a team member had been exclusively allocated to the project. Halfway through the project, the team member was assigned to another project. What should the project manager do?
Options: Consult with functional manager to discuss the impacts to project delivery and develop an appropriate course of action.
Register this as a risk in the risk register because similar situations often occur.
Ensure team members were cross trained to work on the same tasks
Elevate to the project sponsor.
Questions.Explanation: Consult with functional manager to discuss the impacts to project delivery and develop an appropriate course of action It is assumed that the project manager relies on the functional manager for project resource allocation. . The impact of resource reallocation/reassignment should first be discussed with the functional manager. This issue is likely already included in the risk register. There is no indication that team members have not been cross trained, however, these resources are already assigned to other activities and may not be available to take on additional responsibilities. Elevating to the project sponsor is not the initial step to resolve the issue.

Questions.Text: The development of a final project schedule is being delayed by the inability to negotiate an agreement with one of the vendors needed for successful project delivery. What should the project manager do?
Options: Circulate meeting minutes and discuss the agreed and outstanding clauses with the project team and customer.
Meet with the vendor to identify an appropriate path forward; if not possible identify another vendor that can complete the project within acceptable terms.
Involve the project sponsor to seek support with the negotiations.
Communicate the project needs and influence the vendor to accept the terms.
Questions.Explanation: Meet with the vendor to identify an appropriate path forward; if not possible identify another vendor that can complete the project within acceptable terms It is in the best interest of the project to come to terms with the vendor; if not able to do so, the project manager should look for alternative vendors. The other answer choices are incorrect. Circulating the meeting minutes does not resolve the issue and may result in an unfavorable reaction by the customer of the project manager's ability to successfully deliver the project; involving the project sponsor until all other remedies are exhausted is premature; and trying to pressure the vendor my lead to an unacceptable outcome.

Questions.Text: During the last five daily standups, a team member on a hybrid (predictive-agile) project has been vague about what they have done and what they are going to. What should the project manager do next?
Options: Meet with the team member to ensure they understand the accountability responsibilities and to discuss any obstacles.
Review in the retrospective meeting what has been achieved and implement corrective action.
Raise the issue of task accountability with the team and stakeholders at the next iteration review meeting.
Facilitate a meeting with the team and stakeholders to review the risks and impediments faced by the project.
Questions.Explanation: Meet with the team member to ensure they understand the accountability responsibilities and to discuss any obstacles Coaching and mentoring should be used to address the accountability behavior. The question refers to performance issues with one team member, so a one-to-one meeting to surface the issue is the best option. The other answer choices are incorrect because they refer to discussions among the whole team, which is inappropriate here.

Questions.Text: A software delivery team at a large bank is frustrated by the documentation requirements put in place by the governance department in charge of their change control board (CCB). The project manager approaches the governance department and requests that they work together to reduce the amount of paperwork required from the delivery team, explaining that it slows them down and adds overhead costs. This is an example of what kind of leadership?
Options: Servant leadership
Transactional leadership
Directive leadership
Constructive leadership
Questions.Explanation: Servant leadership This is an example of servant leadership because the project manager is offering the service of working with the governance board to resolve an issue for the software delivery team. The project manager has the software delivery team's interest in mind and is willing to work with the governance board to reduce the amount of paperwork.

Questions.Text: An extremely talented and results-oriented team member is suddenly struggling with a personal problem. Due to this, there is a change in the team member's behavior, and it starts to affect the project deliverables as well as the morale of other team members. What should the project manager do?
Options: Assign the team member to another activity.
Empathize to help the team member overcome their problem.
Add the situation to the project issue log.
Ask the human resources (HR) manager to handle the issue.
Questions.Explanation: Empathize to help the team member overcome their problem The project manager's duties include developing and managing the team, in which empathy is an important emotional intelligence characteristic. The project manager should empathize with the team member until the issue is overcome and they can continue working on the deliverable. The other answer choices are incorrect. Assigning the team member to another activity or noting the situation in the issue log does not address the problem. The project manager should attempt to help the team member before involving HR.

Questions.Text: A customer has rejected an interim project deliverable. What should the project manager do?
Options: Review the reason for the rejection, and check the deliverable against its acceptance criteria.
Analyze the customer requirements, and rework the deliverable to an acceptable quality.
Create a deliverable improvement plan, and implement it for the remainder of the project.
Document the rejection of the deliverable in the Lessons Learned log, and review it with the project delivery team.
Questions.Explanation: Review the reason for the rejection, and check the deliverable against its acceptance criteria The reason for customer rejection needs to be established and verified, whether valid or not. This can be done by reviewing the acceptance criteria of the deliverable. The other answer choices are incorrect. Reworking the deliverable, creating an improvement plan, and adding a Lessons Learned entry are premature, as the customer rejection must be investigated first. This question and rationale were developed in reference to: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition (2018) PMI/PMI/5/ [Item Validate Scope is the process of formalizing acceptance of the completed project deliverables. The key benefit of this process is that it brings objectivity to the acceptance process and increases the probability of final product, service, or result acceptance by validating each deliverable. This process is performed periodically throughout the project as needed.]

Questions.Text: In a global, virtual project, the project manager encounters a few issues with team meetings. Some stakeholders do not participate, others complain about the scheduling, and others do not accept the meeting invitation. What should the project manager do to address this concern?
Options: Send a communication to the team's managers and ask for support.
Inform the project sponsor of the situation and ask for support.
Review the organizational culture and structure to properly coordinate meetings.
Reschedule meetings to different times and dates according to time zones.
Questions.Explanation: Review the organizational culture and structure to properly coordinate meetings Prior to meeting coordination with global virtual team, the project manager should review the organizational culture and structure to properly coordinate the meetings and avoid team discomfort and rejection during work. This question and rationale were developed in reference to: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition (2018) PMI/PMI/10.1 Plan Communications Management/371 [Item 10.1.2.3 Communication Technology -Project Environment- Paragraph] | PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition (2018) PMI/PMI/9.3 Acquire Resources/333 [Item 9.3.2.4 Virtual Teams] | The Project Management Tool Kit: 100 Tips and Techniques for Getting the Job Done Right (No Date) /Amazon/33/ [Item Verify that team members and others who should participate will be available, and get their commitment to attend. Face-to-face meetings are best, but for virtual teams, use the best meeting technology available. Set a meeting agenda, including time for: •Positive results: things that went well and practices to repeat •Desirable changes: processes that need improvement or replacement •Prioritization of recommendations •Final thoughts from all contributors Before meeting, collect accurate, up-to-date project documents. For a post-project analysis, ensure that final project reports are available. Also provide access to: •Actual and planned schedule information •Actual and planned resource information •The project integrated change-control history •Issue-management history and problem escalations •Project metrics and performance reports

Questions.Text: Halfway through implementation, the project cosponsor, who is contributing a significant amount of capital into the project, informs the project team of an additional feature to the project deliverable. What should the project manager do?
Options: Assess the impact of the new requirement and update the risk register.
Complete the current project as planned and update it later with the new requirements.
Collect the cosponsor's requirements and perform the integrated change control process.
Convince the cosponsor to maintain the current scope and complete the project as planned.
Questions.Explanation: Collect the cosponsor's requirements and perform the integrated change control process The co-sponsor is a key stakeholder in the project and their requirements should be prioritized. The project manager should collect the requirements and perform the integrated change control process. The other answer choices are incorrect. Updating the risk register happens after the change has been approved; completing the current project without the client's approval or convincing the client that their requirements are not worthwhile negatively impacts project success.

Questions.Text: A project manager has been engaged by a managing director to transform the project management office (PMO) from a waterfall structure focused on software development to an agile PMO. The manager of the PMO has been particularly resistant to the change. Upon investigation, the project manager realizes that the manager's lack of agile knowledge may make them seem incompetent in front of their team of project managers and thus will not support the transformation. How should the project manager proceed?
Options: Ask the managing director to intervene with the PMO manager to explain why this is necessary.
Ask the project team to support the transformation regardless of the PMO manager's negativity.
Implement a change management strategy for all staff to ensure that there is a process which can meet governance requirements.
Implement a training program on agile for all staff to ensure there is congruency of learning for everyone.
Questions.Explanation: Implement a training program on agile for all staff to ensure there is congruency of learning for everyone The project manager should proceed by implementing a training program on agile for all staff, including the manager of the PMO, to ensure there is congruency of learning for everyone. Developing the manager's skills will help reduce resistance to the transition and build consensus. The other answer choices are incorrect. They do not address the lack of skills and the focus in this question is to help staff transition from a waterfall to an agile mindset. It is not good practice to sideline a key stakeholder or defer responsibility by escalating the issue to the managing director.

Questions.Text: A project team member suddenly approaches the project manager and asks to resign from the project. The team member is upset because of poor treatment and consistent complaints from the technical leader. The team member explains that they are no longer willing to tolerate this treatment from the technical leader. The project manager has never had quality issues with the team member. How should the project manager handle this situation?
Options: Allow the team member to resign and replace them since there are additional resources available within the company.
Discuss the situation with the team member to determine why the technical leader has been treating them poorly.
Talk to the technical leader to determine how the team member is poorly performing.
Speak with the team member and technical leader to analyze the conflict and resolve it through mediation.
Questions.Explanation: Speak with the team member and technical leader to analyze the conflict and resolve it through mediation The project manager should mediate between the technical leader and team member to ensure conflict resolution and collaboration. It is important to discuss with both parties to analyze and truly understand the issues they are facing. The other answer choices are incorrect. They propose a one-sided approach to conflict resolution, and allowing the team member to resign without assessing the issue with the technical leader may result in future issues between the technical leader and other team members.

Questions.Text: Project team members inform their project manager of a challenge they are facing. This challenge will likely impact project timelines. What should the project manager do next?
Options: Determine if the challenges brought up by the project team impact the critical path.
Initiate a change request to extend the project timeline.
Work with the team members to understand and evaluate the challenge's impact on the overall project.
Escalate to the project sponsor for proper resolution.
Questions.Explanation: Work with the team members to understand and evaluate the challenge's impact on the overall project The project manager should consider the impact of the challenge and work with the team members to understand and evaluate the impact on the overall project. When any new issue or change request occurs, it is necessary to first understand if it impacts the project and then initiate a change control procedure. The other answer choices are incorrect. The impact on the critical path has already been determined, initiating a change request without first analyzing the impact and escalating to the sponsor without first trying to resolve the situation are not good practices.

Questions.Text: One day prior to starting a task, the technical lead proposed a new implementation approach that can reduce the schedule by 1 week. However, if the approach does not work, the project will be delayed by 3 days. What should the project manager do next?
Options: Request a change order to reduce the schedule by 1 week.
Inform the technical lead that the proposal will not work.
Refer to the network diagram to determine the float for this task.
Communicate the possible 3-day project delay to the sponsor.
Questions.Explanation: Refer to the network diagram to determine the float for this task. Considering this is a change which will directly impact the schedule, the project manager needs to determine if there is a spare time(float/slack) for this task and if any, see if it is less than, same as or more than the additional 3 days the task will require if the new approach fails. Other options provided are important but wouldn't be the NEXT step to take as the question requested. This question and rationale were developed in reference to: Mastering Project Time Management, Cost Control, and Quality Management (No Date) Randal Wilson//Project Activity Sequencing/ [Item Float/slack calculation—Once the forward and backward pass processes have been completed and all early start, early finish, late start, and late finish values have been recorded on each node, a simple calculation can be made to determine if there is slack (spare time) available for each work activity. Using this information, the project manager can determine the earliest possible start and finish dates as well as the latest possible start and finish dates and whether activities have spare time available. The calculation for slack can utilize information from either start or finish values that will typically yield the same slack value.] | PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition (2018) PMI/PMI/6/6.5.2.2 CRITICAL PATH METHOD [Item The critical path method is used to estimate the minimum project duration and determine the amount of schedule flexibility on the logical network paths within the schedule model. ]

Questions.Text: A project has a budgeted cost of $120,000. According to the schedule, 15% of the project should have been completed after one month. After a month, only 12% of the project has actually been completed. What is the scheduled performance index (SPI)?
Questions.Explanation: 0.8 The planned value is: $120,000 * 15% = $18,000. The earned value is: $120,000 * 12% = $14,400. The SPI is equal to earned value divided by planed value: SPI = EV / PV = 14,400 / 18,000 = 0.8. This means that for every estimated hour of work, the project team is only completing 0.8 hours, or 48 minutes. Since the SPI is less than 1 then the project is running behind schedule. If the ratio has a value higher than 1 this indicates the project is progressing well against the schedule. If the SPI is 1, then the project is progressing exactly as planned. This question and rationale were developed in reference to: Effective Project Management: Traditional, Agile, Extreme, Hybrid (No Date) Robert K. Wysocki//CHAPTER 9 How to Execute a TPM Project/ | PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition (2018) PMI/PMI/7.4.2.2/267 [Item Schedule Performance Index (SPI). A measure of schedule efficiency expressed as the ratio of earned value to planned value.]

Questions.Text: A project team is unexpectedly audited. Several non-conformances are identified during the audit. Which three actions could the project manager have taken to prevent this situation? (Choose three)
Options: Requested advance notification of the audit
Anticipated nonconformance items
Requested that the team prepare for a possible audit
Ensured that the auditors are experienced
Rejected the audit request
Questions.Explanation: Requested advance notification of the audit, Anticipated nonconformance items and Requested that the team prepare for a possible audit Audits are a part of the process. The team is expected to be in compliance and maintain products and deliverables as per project standards. The project manager could possibly request advance notice of the audit to be better prepared to correct nonconformance items. The other answer choices are incorrect. They are outside of the project manager's authority and ability.

Questions.Text: A project manager is beginning a new project and is starting to form the project team. The project manager has not worked with anyone from this team previously, and most of the team members do not know each other. Some of the team members are located in different countries. Which two actions should the project manager take to help the team work more closely together? (Choose two)
Options: Identify the cultural differences and potential conflict areas to mitigate these going forward.
Allocate a budget for the team members to temporarily colocate for the duration of the project.
Arrange virtual team-building sessions that allow team members to get to know each other.
Select team members who have previously worked together to be allocated to the project.
Identify experienced team members to provide mentoring for the less experienced team members.
Questions.Explanation: The project manager should identify cultural differences and potential conflict areas to ensure that issue do not arise. They should also organize virtual team building activities to encourage team rapport. The other answer choices are incorrect. There is an assumption that there is budget and willingness to collocate team members; there is also an assumption that some team members have worked together and are more experienced than others.

Questions.Text: A customer reviewed a product and requested additional features that were not in the requirements documentation. The work in progress (WIP) limits have already been optimized for the teams working on different work components. How should the project manager begin to resolve the customer requirements?
Options: Build the product according to the previously agreed customer requirements.
Obtain details for the new items and submit them to the Change Control Board.
Breakdown work on additional features with existing component teams.
Release increments of the new items for customer validation.
Questions.Explanation: Obtain details for the new items and submit them to the Change Control Board The project manager should obtain further details on the new items, reprioritize them, and submit them to the Change Control Board for approval while maintaining the agreed WIP limit. The other answer choices are incorrect. Building to the previously agreed customer requirements is not valid since the customer has requested and is expecting the additional features. Breaking down the work and releasing incremental product versions for the customer are all valid options but are expected as part of the delivery process and does not immediately resolve the new customer requirements.

Questions.Text: A key member of a project team publicly asserts that another team member has been speaking negatively about them. The team member threatens to quit their job if the project manager does not take immediate action to address the conflict. What should the project manager do first?
Options: Call a team meeting because the project is in jeopardy without the key team member.
Meet with the accused team member to understand their point of view.
Schedule a meeting in the future to allow the issue to diffuse over time.
Decide to remove one of the two members off the team to address the conflict.
Questions.Explanation: Meet with the accused team member to understand their point of view The project manager should meet with the accused team member to understand more about the situation first before attempting to start resolving this conflict. The other answer choices are incorrect. Calling a team meeting is not effective as the other team members are not involved in the conflict between the two members; the option to remove one of the members will jeopardize the work delivery without resolving the conflict; and scheduling a meeting in the future does not address the current issue and conflicts should be addressed immediately.

Questions.Text: An agile team has members in several countries. Daily meetings are conducted in a common language over a conference call, and the project manager takes notes to track work progress. Differences in pronunciation and vocabulary make it extremely difficult for members to understand each other. What should the project manager do to improve project efficiency?
Options: Use the call to verbally summarize the work status as reported by members.
Use an electronic Kanban board to give a visual summary of the work progress for each member.
Ask for a change in the project's budget to provide access to a real-time translation service so members can listen to the meeting in their own language.
Ask team members to report daily on the work completed, work to do, and blockers, which will be sent to the team's email distribution list.
Questions.Explanation: Use an electronic Kanban board to give a visual summary of the work progress for each member The project manager should use an electronic Kanban board to give a visual summary of the work progress for each member. The Kanban board will provide a common source of truth and will eliminate difficult pronunciations. The daily meeting should be maintained to allow team members to express themselves which is fundamental in agile. The other answer choices are incorrect. Summarizing or directing work is against agile principles. Real-time translation can introduce further information distortion.

Questions.Text: A project manager is working on a project to modernize a bank's information technology (IT) infrastructure. The project manager recently received approval of the project's change management plan from the project sponsor. The project sponsor now requests that the project manager implement a new piece of technology in the solution. What should the project manager do next?
Options: Add the new technology as a risk in the risk register.
Submit a change request to the change control board (CCB).
Update the schedule and cost baselines to document the new technology.
Request the project sponsor to procure the new technology.
Questions.Explanation: Submit a change request to the change control board (CCB) The project manager should first complete a change request and submit it to the CCB. The other answer choices are incorrect as they are not the first choices for the project manager. Based on the impact analysis and CCB approval, the project manager could then add the new technology as a risk in the risk register, update the schedule and cost baselines to document the new technology, and finally request the project sponsor to procure the new technology.

Questions.Text: In order to reduce procurement time during a bidding process, a project manager decided to contract the additional work to a current contractor. The other bidders are complaining about this decision. What should the project manager have done to prevent this?
Options: Invited all bidders to a meeting
Awarded the contract without negotiation
Followed the company's bidding procedure
Shared the project objectives with all bidders
Questions.Explanation: Followed the company's bidding procedure It is very important for the project manager to ensure that there is complete transparency in the bidding process. This can be possible only if the project manager followed the company's bidding procedure. Hence this option is the correct answer. The other answer choices are incorrect. Inviting all bidders to a meeting or sharing the project objectives with all bidders could perhaps be one of the step in the bidding process, but this does not address other bidders' unhappiness. Awarding the contract without negotiation is a distractor as it may deviate from the company's bidding procedure.

Questions.Text: During a software development project, the team realizes that several features failed to comply with quality requirements. After receiving instructions to fix the issues, one team member becomes really frustrated, claiming that unclear requirements are causing the issues. Other team members argue that negligent testing is the real cause of these issues. The testers blame poor coding quality. What should the project manager do?
Options: Support the project team in a retrospective to identify the root cause of the quality issues.
Remove the most frustrated resource from the team as the confusion is impacting team motivation.
Perform a quality review in each stage of the development life cycle to identify the reason for quality issues.
Discuss the issue with the management team to identify possible solutions to improve quality.
Questions.Explanation: Support the project team in a retrospective to identify the root cause of the quality issues The project team is initially responsible for conflict resolution, but the project manager can facilitate it when required. Retrospectives can address the issue, thus the project manager should support the project team in a retrospective to identify the root cause of the quality issues.

Questions.Text: The stakeholder engagement assessment matrix governs the project management team’s stakeholder management activities during project execution. One stakeholder knows about the project but is not supportive. What type of stakeholder is this?
Options: Resistant
Leading
Neutral
Supportive
Questions.Explanation: Resistant Resistant - They are aware of the project but not in support of it. Neutral - They are aware of the project but have no opinion regarding their support for it. Supportive - They are supportive of the project and wish it to succeed. Leading - They are actively engaged in project success and willing to lend assistance to help it succeed. Unaware - They are not aware of the project and its potential impacts on them.

Questions.Text: A majority portion of a local company was recently sold to an international company. The new parent company is mandating the use of health, safety, and environmental (HSE) regulations for all projects in the local company. What should the project managers do to remain compliant?
Options: Wait for the functional department responsible for HSE regulations to communicate the process change.
Ask the functional department responsible for HSE regulations to assign a new team member.
Speak with the project sponsor to get clarification about the new HSE regulations.
Meet with the HSE team to understand the impact of the new regulations on the projects.
Questions.Explanation: Meet with the HSE team to understand the impact of the new regulations on the projects. To remain compliant, the project manager should meet with the HSE team to understand the impact of the new regulations on the projects.

Questions.Text: A project manager is finishing a project and sponsor is requesting insights on benefits realization. How can the project manager calculate project outcomes value?
Options: Evaluate qualitative and quantitative project benefits.
Price tangible benefits and divide them by expenses.
Conduct a retrospective financial assessment.
Assess outcomes and reduce them from the budget.
Questions.Explanation: Evaluate qualitative and quantitative project benefits. The project benefits management plan describes the mechanisms that are in place to measure the project benefits. A project benefit is the outcome of actions, behaviors, products, services, or results that provide value to the sponsoring organization. Metrics are used to show realized benefits, direct and indirect measures, which means they can be obtained by evaluating both qualitative and quantitative outcomes. Normally benefits are measured as value delivered to organizations expressed as Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR) our Benefit minus Cost. Benefits are not only tangible outcomes, neither connected with financial aspects. Benefits minus Cost (not budget) is Value delivered.

Questions.Text: A large-scale, multiyear transformation project was completed as per the defined requirements and within the approved budget and schedule. The requirements were also validated and confirmed by the business. However, the post-delivery analysis of the project revealed that the project did not provide benefits as expected. What should the project manager have done to avoid this?
Options: Ensured the requirements were properly captured in the product backlog
Created a business strategy to assist with organizational change management
Improved the status reporting processes to highlight project results
Measured and demonstrated business value throughout the project
Questions.Explanation: Measured and demonstrated business value throughout the project To avoid this, the project manager should have measured and demonstrated business value throughout the project.

Questions.Text: A new regulation has been approved that will impact the deliverables in the fourth phase of the project. The delivery is following an iterative approach and is currently completing the second phase. What should the project manager do next?
Options: Consult with the project sponsor to discuss a cost increase.
Get approval for the additional budget from the steering committee.
Meet with subject matter experts (SMEs) to assess the impact to objectives.
Acquire approval for an exception to the implementation of the new regulation.
Questions.Explanation: Meet with subject matter experts (SMEs) to assess the impact to objectives. "Meet with subject matter experts (SMEs) to assess the impact to objectives" is the best choice as impacts in cost and scheduling, resourcing must be assessed to understand the change(s) required as per the new regulation. "Consult with the project sponsor..." this could be one of the steps but not the immediate next step. "Get approval for..." this could be one of the steps but not the immediate next step. "Acquire approval..." this could be one of the steps but not the immediate next step.

Questions.Text: A new customer relations data base is being deployed. The schedule calls for migration from the previous system to occur over one weekend. However some key stakeholders believe it may take more time for the mitigation. i What should the project manager do?
Options: Assess the concerns and risks of not deploying within the scheduled time frame and with stakeholder concurrence act accordingly.
Change the deployment plan such that additional resources are available to perform the weekend deployment.
Review and revise as necessary the risk management plan
Modify the schedule to increase the time frame of the deployment plan.
Questions.Explanation: Assess the concerns and risks of not deploying within the scheduled time frame and with stakeholder concurrence act accordingly. The project manager should assess the risk in accordance with the risk management plan and act accordingly hanging the deployment plan by adding resources may not address the stakeholder requirements Revising the risk management plan will not address stakeholder concerns It may not be necessary to modify the deployment plan

Questions.Text: After several interviews with key stakeholders and checking the project charter, a project manager found that the current project organizational structure is no longer appropriate for the project's strategic goals. What should the project manager do next?
Options: Assess the lessons learned documents and validate which organizational structure was used in the past for similar projects.
Ask the team to document the risk in the project risk register and develop a risk response plan accordingly.
Evaluate the gaps between the current organizational structure and the expected project benefits to determine required actions.
Ask the team to review the project charter and evaluate the project objectives for relevance.
Questions.Explanation: Evaluate the gaps between the current organizational structure and the expected project benefits to determine required actions. Organizational structure should be flexible to accommodate the project goal, and may evolve in the project life cycle. If the current structure is not appropriate, the project manager should first evaluate the deficiencies against what the project needs to accomplish to determine a more fitting organizational structure. Assessing lessons learned from prior projects may give some direction, but gaps on the current project should be evaluated first. There is nothing in the question that suggests that project objectives are incorrect, so evaluating the objectives for relevance is not warranted. This question and rationale were developed in reference to: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition (2018) PMI/PMI/9. Project Resource Management/ [Item Projects with high variability benefit from team structures that maximize focus and collaboration, such as self-organizing teams with generalizing specialists. Collaboration is intended to boost productivity and facilitate innovative problem solving.
